name: Umayr ibn Sa'd al-Ansari
title: Umayr ibn Sa'd al-Ansari (RA): One of a Kind
meta_description: The story of Umayr ibn Sa'd al-Ansari (RA): the orphan boy of Madinah always in the first row of prayer, who chose his faith over the man who raised him, and grew into one of the most trusted governors of his age.
keywords: Umayr ibn Sa'd, Umayr ibn Sad al-Ansari, story of Umayr, governor of Homs, the Firsts, integrity in Islam, sahaba stories, hypocrites of Madinah
lede: He was a boy in Madinah who loved the front row of prayer. He did not say much, and he asked for nothing. Years later a caliph would walk out to meet him and find a governor who had arrived on foot, sunburnt and blistered, carrying everything he owned in a worn pouch.

This is the story of Umayr ibn Sa'd al-Ansari (may Allah be pleased with him): an orphan raised under the shade of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, who learned integrity as a child and never sold it, not even when the cost was the man who had been a father to him.

sections.h2: An orphan in the new city
paras: Umayr was one of the children of the Ansar: the young people of Madinah who met the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ as boys and grew up shaped by his company. His own father, Sa'd, was a companion who fought at Badr, among the most honoured of the believers, and died not long after. So Umayr was left an orphan young.

His mother then married a man named Julas ibn Suwayd. Julas was one of the seniors of the tribe: wealthy, influential, counted among the intelligent men of the city. He raised Umayr with real love, and to the boy he was, in every way that mattered, a father.

Umayr was about ten years old when the Prophet ﷺ came to Madinah, and he embraced Islam himself. There is not much recorded of his conversations with the Prophet ﷺ. What the biographers noted instead was simpler, and somehow more telling: this child was always in the first row of prayer.

sections.h2: The boy in the front row
paras: He came to the mosque with or without the man who was raising him, and he kept coming. His mother was pleased to see it. The Prophet ﷺ noticed it. It became the thing people remembered about him.

There is a well-known hadith about seven people who will be shaded by Allah on the Day of Judgment, and one of them is a young person who grew up attached to the mosque. Some of the scholars of hadith name Umayr as an example of exactly that. He was not loud about his faith. He simply could not be kept away from the prayer.

His path runs close to others in this series: orphans of the Ansar like Zayd ibn Harithah and Usamah, young people who met the Prophet ﷺ early and grew up in his care. They were not famous for great speeches. They were known for being present, in the prayer and later in the ranks, and for a loyalty that did not need announcing.
It was a quiet beginning, the kind that leaves no dramatic story behind it. But something was being formed in those years that the rest of his life would lean on.

sections.h2: A word that could not be unheard
paras: When the Prophet ﷺ called the people to the hard expedition of Tabuk, the hypocrites of Madinah held back and made every excuse they could find. Umayr was still too young to fight, but he was in the mosque watching. He saw believers come forward with everything they had, one man trying even to sell his own bed so he could go out and stand beside the Prophet ﷺ.

Umayr went home and told Julas to come forward the way these men were coming forward, to give and to go. Julas answered with words that should never have left his mouth. He said that if Muhammad ﷺ was telling the truth, then they were worse than donkeys. It was a statement of disbelief, and it exposed him.

Julas had drifted in among the believers without ever truly believing. Like others in those years, he had played both sides, waiting to see who would prevail, settling himself among the Muslims while keeping his heart elsewhere. The word he spoke gave him away.

It put the boy in an impossible place. Julas had been good to him. Umayr said to him, by Allah, there was no one on the face of the earth after the Prophet ﷺ more beloved to him than Julas. But he had just heard a word that, if he repeated it, would expose the man he loved, and if he buried it, would betray his religion.

Umayr carried it to the Prophet ﷺ, because his first responsibility was there. The Prophet ﷺ called Julas, and Julas denied everything: he never said it, the boy made it up, you know how young people are. It was now one word against another. The Prophet ﷺ stayed silent, and Umayr, heartbroken, began to weep and to beg Allah to make the truth plain.

The Prophet ﷺ could not punish on one word against another, and he did not. He waited. He left the matter where matters like it belong, with Allah, and Umayr could only wait with him, his heart breaking over a man he had loved and feared he had wronged.
Then a verse of Surah at-Tawbah came down about the man who had sworn a false oath. It confirmed exactly what Umayr had said, and it left a door open that Umayr could not have opened himself.
The Prophet ﷺ turned to him and told him that his ears had been truthful in what they heard, and that his Lord had confirmed what he said.

ayah.text: They swear by God that they did not, but they certainly did speak words of defiance and became defiant after having submitted; they tried to do something, though they did not achieve it, being spiteful was their only response to God and His Messenger enriching them out of His bounty. They would be better off turning back [to God]: if they turn away, God will punish them in this world and the Hereafter, and there will be no one on earth to protect or help them.

sections.h2: The door that was left open
paras: The verse did not only expose. It said that turning back to Allah would be better for them, and Julas was one of those who took that door. He repented, and afterward he used to weep and say, may Allah reward Umayr for saving me from the Fire.
He understood what had happened. Had the boy stayed quiet, Julas might have died in that state and lost everything. Because a child refused to hide the truth, a man was given back his chance.

There is a saying remembered about Umayr that gathers his whole life into one line: whoever knows Allah in their youth will not sell their religion in their old age. He knew Allah young. He built his integrity early, and he kept it.

sections.h2: The governor who arrived on foot
paras: Years later, under the caliph Umar ibn al-Khattab, that same quiet boy was a man of trust. He fought in the campaigns in Sham and settled in Syria, and Umar, who valued exactly his kind of integrity, made him governor of Damascus and then of Homs. Homs was a place of great wealth, the kind of post where a man could quietly help himself, and Umar chose for it men who could not be bought.

Umayr governed it like a fortress. He once stood and said that Islam is a fortified wall and a secure gate: the wall is its justice, and the gate is its truth. Break the wall and the gate is smashed and the breach is open. Strength, he said, does not come from the sword or the whip, but from judging by truth and taking only with justice.

For a whole year Umar heard no complaint from Homs and received nothing for the treasury, and he grew curious. He sent for Umayr. The governor took a worn pouch, a bowl, a cup, and his spear, and he walked the entire road to Madinah on foot. He arrived sunburnt and blistered, barely standing.

Umar asked what had happened to him. Umayr said he was healthy, that he had his pouch for his food and a vessel to drink and wash from, and that as far as he was concerned he had the whole world. He had collected the people's wealth carefully and given it back to them in their rights, and that was why there was nothing left to send and nothing to complain about.

sections.h2: Storing it for a day worth more
paras: Umar wanted to be sure. He sent a man named al-Harith to Homs with a sum of money and told him to stay as a guest: if Umayr was living comfortably, keep the money, but if his life was as hard as it looked, give him all of it. Al-Harith stayed three nights and watched a family that gave away its bread to others and went hungry rather than keep it.

He offered Umayr the money. Umayr refused it and said he had no need of it. It was his wife who finally said to take it, and to keep only what they needed and give the rest to the poor. So Umayr took it, and went straight out and gave it away, beginning with the poor and then the children of the martyrs, saying you can never honour them enough.

When Umar later asked what he had done with the money, Umayr's answer was simple. He had sent it ahead for himself, for the day when no wealth and no children would benefit a person. He kept only what his family truly needed, and even the cloth he was offered he set aside for his wife, who had been tested by the same hardship he had.

Umar had wanted to renew his post, but Umayr asked to be released; the trust of governing weighed on him more than any honour it carried. When he passed away, Umar said that if only he had more men like him to govern the affairs of the Muslims.

lessons_intro: Almost nothing dramatic is recorded about him. What we have is a boy who loved the prayer and a man who could not be bought, and a straight line running between the two.

lessons.text: Whoever knows Allah in their youth will not sell their religion in their old age. The trust Umayr was famous for as a governor was the same trust he learned as a child in the front row.

lessons.bold: Truth is worth more than comfort.
lessons.text: He told the truth knowing it could cost him the man he loved most after the Prophet ﷺ. He did not let love for a person override his duty to what was right.

lessons.bold: Telling the truth can save, not only expose.
lessons.text: Because Umayr refused to stay quiet, Julas was given the chance to repent. Honesty offered in good faith is sometimes the door back for the very person it exposes.
He sought no position and kept nothing for himself, giving the people their rights and storing the rest for a day he believed was worth far more.

faq.q: Who was Umayr ibn Sa'd al-Ansari?
faq.a: A companion of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ from the Ansar of Madinah. Orphaned young and raised by his stepfather, he embraced Islam as a child and later became a trusted governor of Damascus and then Homs under the caliph Umar ibn al-Khattab.

faq.q: Why is Umayr called one of a kind?
faq.a: It was the title Umar ibn al-Khattab gave him. The Arabic image is of a uniquely woven garment, something with no equal. Umar used it for Umayr because of his rare integrity as a governor who could not be corrupted.

faq.q: What is the story behind the verse in Surah at-Tawbah?
faq.a: As a boy, Umayr heard his stepfather Julas ibn Suwayd speak words of disbelief during the time of Tabuk. He reported it to the Prophet ﷺ, Julas swore a false oath denying it, and a verse came down confirming Umayr's word and leaving the door of repentance open. Julas later repented.

faq.q: What can we learn from the life of Umayr ibn Sa'd?
faq.a: That integrity is formed early and kept for life, that the truth is worth more than comfort or status, and that honesty can be a mercy even to the person it exposes.

closing_heading: Why this story stays with us
closing: Most of us will never govern a city or stand in the path of a famous verse. But every ordinary day asks the same small question Umayr kept answering: will you tell the truth when it costs you something, and will you keep what is not yours, or give it back. His answers were never loud. They were just steady, again and again, until they became who he was.

He learned to love the prayer before he understood what it would protect him from, and that quiet habit held the rest of his life in place. There is an invitation in that for any of us: to build something honest now, in private, before it is ever tested, so that when the test comes we already know which way we will turn.
source_note: The narrative follows the account of Umayr ibn Sa'd al-Ansari (RA) in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts (Yaqeen Institute). The Qur'an translation is from The Qur'an, M.A.S. Abdel Haleem (Qur'an 9:74). Where the historical record carries more than one narration, we have followed the most widely reported.

slides:
He was a boy in Madinah who could not be kept away from the front row of prayer.

He said little. He asked for nothing.

Years later, a caliph would call him one of a kind.

Orphaned young, Umayr was raised by his stepfather, Julas: wealthy, respected, and to the boy, truly a father.

He loved him. That love was about to be tested.

When the call came to march for Tabuk, the hypocrites made excuses.

Umayr watched believers give everything. One man tried to sell his own bed to go.

Then Julas said it: if Muhammad ﷺ is telling the truth, we are worse than donkeys.

The boy now held a word that could not be unheard.

Repeat it, and he exposes the man he loves most after the Prophet ﷺ.

Bury it, and he betrays his faith.

He chose the truth. He told the Prophet ﷺ.

Julas swore he never said it.

Umayr wept and begged Allah for the truth. Then a verse of Surah at-Tawbah came down, and confirmed every word the boy had spoken.

**Whoever knows Allah in their youth will not sell their religion in their old age.**

As governor of Homs, he walked to Madinah on foot, owning only a worn pouch and a cup.

He gave the people their rights and kept nothing for himself.

"I sent it ahead, for the day when no wealth will benefit a person."

May Allah be pleased with him.
social.caption:
Umayr ibn Sa'd al-Ansari (may Allah be pleased with him) was an orphan boy in Madinah, known for one thing: he was always in the first row of prayer.

As a child, he overheard the man who raised him speak a word of disbelief. Telling the truth would expose the person he loved most after the Prophet ﷺ. Staying silent would betray his faith. He chose the truth, and a verse of the Qur'an came down confirming his word and leaving a door open for the man to repent.

That same integrity made him, years later, a governor who could not be bought, who walked to Madinah on foot and gave the people's wealth back to them.

Which part of his story stayed with you? 🤍

Save this for later, and share it with someone who would love it.

Read the full story: buruja.com/companions/umayr-ibn-sad

email.subject: The boy who told the truth, even when it cost him
email.alt_subject: "Your ears were truthful in what they heard"
email.preview: An orphan in Madinah, always in the front row of prayer, who would not sell his faith for anything.
email.body:
Assalamu alaikum,

Umayr ibn Sa'd al-Ansari (may Allah be pleased with him) was an orphan boy in Madinah. The biographers do not record many of his conversations with the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ. What they remembered instead was simple: this child was always in the first row of prayer.

He was raised by a stepfather named Julas, a wealthy and respected man who loved him and whom he loved in return. Then, around the time of the expedition to Tabuk, Umayr heard Julas say a word of plain disbelief.

It put the boy in an impossible place. If he repeated the word, he would expose the man who was like a father to him. If he buried it, he would betray his religion. He told the Prophet ﷺ, because his first duty was there.

Julas swore he never said it. It was now one word against another, and the Prophet ﷺ stayed silent. Umayr wept and begged Allah to make the truth plain. Then a verse of Surah at-Tawbah came down, confirming exactly what the boy had said, and the Prophet ﷺ told him that his ears had been truthful in what they heard.

The verse did more than expose. It left a door open, and Julas walked through it. He repented, and afterward he used to say, may Allah reward Umayr for saving me from the Fire. A boy's honesty had become a man's second chance.

There is a line remembered about Umayr that holds his whole life: whoever knows Allah in their youth will not sell their religion in their old age. He learned integrity as a child and kept it. Years later, as a governor under Umar, he could not be bought. He walked to his caliph on foot, owning almost nothing, having given the people back their wealth and kept none of it for himself. "I sent it ahead," he said, "for the day when no wealth will benefit a person."

May Allah be pleased with him, and make us honest when honesty is hard.

If you would like the full story, we have written it here:

paras: Umayr ibn Sa'd al-Ansari (may Allah be pleased with him) was a boy in Madinah, one of the children of the Ansar who grew up under the shade of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ. His father had fought at Badr and died young, so Umayr was raised by his stepfather, Julas, a wealthy and respected man who loved him like his own.

The biographers did not record many of his words. They remembered something quieter instead: that this child was always in the first row of prayer. With or without the man who raised him, he came to the mosque, and he kept coming.

Then came the hard call to march for Tabuk, and Umayr heard Julas say a word of plain disbelief. The boy was caught. Julas was the most beloved person to him after the Prophet ﷺ. To repeat the word would expose him. To hide it would betray his faith.

He chose the truth and carried it to the Prophet ﷺ. Julas swore he had never said it, and it became one word against another. Umayr wept and begged Allah to make the truth plain. Then a verse of Surah at-Tawbah came down and confirmed every word the boy had spoken, and the Prophet ﷺ told him his ears had been truthful in what they heard.

The verse left a door open, and Julas walked through it. He repented, and afterward he would weep and say, may Allah reward Umayr for saving me from the Fire. A boy's honesty had become a man's second chance.

Years later, under the caliph Umar, that same quiet boy was a governor who could not be bought. When Umar called him from Homs, Umayr walked the whole road on foot, sunburnt and blistered, owning little more than a worn pouch and a cup, and said that as far as he was concerned he had the whole world.

He had given the people their wealth back in full and kept none of it. "I sent it ahead," he said, "for the day when no wealth will benefit a person." When he died, Umar wished aloud that he had more men like him.
storybook.pullquote: Whoever knows Allah in their youth will not sell their religion in their old age.
storybook.reflection: Umayr's life is a straight line drawn from a boy in the front row to a man who could not be bought. He built his integrity in private, long before it was ever tested, so that when the hardest moment came he already knew which way he would turn. The quiet habits we keep now, when no one is watching, are the ones that hold us later.
storybook.source: His story follows the account in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts.
